_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan Part B (paragraph matching), batch A2 REWORKED to 450-650 words. 7 labelled paragraphs (A-G), each 5-7 sentences; 5 statements are strong paraphrases (not literal lifts). answers = 0-based indices; paras=7. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage:
[A] Few phenomena in modern medicine are as genuinely puzzling as the placebo effect, in which a treatment containing no active ingredient nonetheless makes real patients feel measurably better. It has been observed for as long as medicine has been studied, yet it stubbornly resists any easy explanation. Far from being a mere curiosity at the edge of research, it sits awkwardly at the very centre of how we test whether any new drug truly works. To understand it is to confront just how deeply the mind is woven into the workings of the body.

[B] At its simplest, a placebo is a dummy: a sugar pill, a saline injection or a sham procedure containing nothing that should, by the ordinary rules of chemistry, do anything at all. The expectation, on paper, is plainly that it will have no effect whatsoever. And yet, in carefully controlled trial after carefully controlled trial, such dummies produce a small but genuine and measurable improvement in patients, one that cannot simply be dismissed as imagination or as wishful reporting.

[C] Part of the explanation lies in expectation itself. Believing, rightly or wrongly, that one is being treated can prompt the brain to release its own pain-relieving chemicals, producing changes that are not imagined but physical and real. The anticipation of relief, it turns out, is enough to set in motion a cascade of measurable activity inside the nervous system, almost as though the body were quietly preparing to heal in advance of any actual medicine.

[D] The surrounding ritual matters too, often surprisingly much. Larger pills, brightly coloured capsules, injections and elaborate, solemn procedures tend to produce noticeably stronger effects than a plain white tablet handed over without any ceremony. The more impressive and authoritative the treatment appears to the patient, the more powerfully the placebo response seems to follow, suggesting that we respond not only to the substance itself but to the whole theatre of being cared for.

[E] This strange power raises a genuine ethical problem for medicine. If placebos work, at least partly, through belief, can a doctor honestly prescribe one without deceiving the very patient he is trying to help? Deliberately fostering a false belief, however kindly meant and however beneficial its results may be, sits uncomfortably with the duty of plain honesty that lies at the heart of the relationship between physician and patient.

[F] Remarkably, recent studies suggest that such deception may not actually be required after all. Patients told quite openly and truthfully that they are taking a placebo, with no active drug inside it whatsoever, have still, in a number of cases, gone on to report real and lasting relief from their symptoms. The mere act of taking something, within the reassuring ritual of being treated, appears to carry some quiet power of its own.

[G] Whatever the precise underlying mechanism finally proves to be, the placebo effect stands as a permanent and humbling reminder that mind and body are far less separate than our everyday assumptions, and much of our medicine, have long encouraged us to believe.
statements: Some patients have reported genuine relief even when openly and truthfully informed that what they took contained no real medicine.
By the ordinary rules of chemistry, a dummy treatment holds nothing that ought to produce any effect at all.
The more elaborate and ceremonious a treatment appears, the stronger its effect tends to be.
Giving a patient a dummy pill provokes a moral unease about whether they are being misled.
The mere expectation of being treated can prompt the brain to produce its own natural painkillers.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(公开告知是安慰剂仍好转) S2→B(化学上不含应起效成分) S3→D(治疗越隆重效果越强) S4→E(开安慰剂的欺骗伦理担忧) S5→C(期望促使大脑释放天然镇痛物)。A/G 未匹配。强同义改写。

sets.passage:
[A] The great and abiding weakness of solar and wind power has always been their sheer inconstancy: the sun reliably sets and the wind unpredictably drops, often at precisely the moments when demand for electricity is at its highest. A grid cannot run on power that arrives only when the weather happens to oblige. For this reason, the problem of storing energy has moved to the very centre of any serious vision of a clean-power future, and may matter more than generation itself.

[B] Batteries are the most familiar answer, and the one most people think of first. The price of lithium cells has fallen dramatically over the past decade, making them far cheaper than they once were, yet their storage capacity remains stubbornly limited. They are well suited to smoothing out the gaps of hours, but ill-suited to holding power across the weeks or seasons during which the wind may stay calm and the skies overcast.

[C] An older and humbler method still quietly does much of the heavy work. Surplus electricity, generated when supply outstrips demand, can be used to pump great volumes of water uphill into a high reservoir, and that water can later be released downhill through turbines to regenerate power when it is needed. The approach is proven and efficient, but it depends entirely on having the right mountainous landscape, which most places simply lack.

[D] Hydrogen offers another and much-discussed route. Spare electricity can be used to split water into hydrogen gas, which can then be stored and later burned or recombined to release its energy on demand. The appeal is real, but so is the drawback: a considerable fraction of the original energy is irretrievably lost at each stage of conversion, making the whole cycle markedly less efficient than it first appears.

[E] Sometimes, however, the cheapest storage of all is simply to use less at the wrong moments. By gently shifting when people run their dishwashers, heat their homes or charge their electric cars, the peaks and troughs of demand itself can be flattened out to match the available supply, reducing the amount of storage that has to be built in the first place.

[F] None of these solutions, it must be said, is free. Storage is what makes intermittent renewable energy genuinely reliable, but it can only be provided through heavy and sustained investment that someone, whether taxpayer or consumer, must in the end actually pay for. The clean grid will not store its own power for nothing.

[G] The transition to clean energy, then, will be decided less by how cleverly we learn to generate power than by how cleverly, and how affordably, we learn to keep it until the moment it is wanted.
statements: Splitting water to store hydrogen forfeits a significant share of the energy at every conversion.
Although lithium cells have grown much cheaper, they still hold too little for storage over weeks.
Making storage work depends on heavy investment that someone must ultimately fund.
Pumping water uphill to store energy works only where the landscape is suitable.
Shifting the times at which people use power can flatten demand to fit the available supply.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(电解制氢每次转换大量损耗) S2→B(锂电池更便宜但容量不足以跨周储存) S3→F(储能需大量投资且须有人买单) S4→C(抽水蓄能需合适地形) S5→E(调整用电时间使需求匹配供给)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] As keyboards and touchscreens steadily replace pens, handwriting is quietly fading from daily life. Some welcome the change as the natural and overdue retirement of an outdated skill; others fear that in abandoning the pen we may be losing something more valuable than mere penmanship. The debate is not really about nostalgia, but about what, if anything, the slow physical act of writing by hand does for the mind that typing does not.

[B] The practical case for typing is undeniably strong. It is faster than handwriting for most people, far neater and more legible, effortlessly corrected, and instantly searched once stored. Few professions still demand elegant penmanship, and a generation raised on keyboards can produce in minutes what once took painstaking hours, so it is hardly surprising that the pen has been pushed to the margins of working life.

[C] Yet a body of research suggests a hidden cost to this convenience. Students who take lecture notes by hand, being physically unable to record every single word as fast as it is spoken, are forced to listen, select and summarise as they write. This effortful processing, far from being a disadvantage, means they tend to understand the material more deeply and to remember it considerably better than those who type a near-complete transcript without thinking.

[D] Handwriting may aid the young in other ways still. The slow, deliberate work of forming each letter by hand appears to help small children learn to read, engaging the brain in a way that tapping an identical key for every letter simply does not. In tracing the shapes themselves, children seem to build a richer mental image of the letters they will later need to recognise instantly on the page.

[E] There is a cultural and emotional dimension as well. A handwritten letter carries a personal weight that no typed email can quite match, precisely because it visibly takes time, bears the unmistakable marks of an individual hand, and cannot be copied and mass-produced at the press of a button. Its very inefficiency is what makes it feel like a genuine gift of attention.

[F] Critics of such nostalgia reply, fairly enough, that every technology of writing was once new and once distrusted, including the humble pen itself, which ancient thinkers feared would weaken memory. Each generation, they note, mourns the tool it grew up with, and the world has nonetheless gone on thinking and feeling perfectly well.

[G] The likeliest future, then, is not the outright death of handwriting but its gradual narrowing into something more deliberate: a skill kept and treasured for the particular moments when slowness, effort and personality still genuinely matter. The pen, in this account, will survive not as an everyday working tool but as a deliberate and meaningful choice, valued precisely for the time, care and individuality it visibly demands of the writer.
statements: Every technology of writing, the pen included, was at first regarded as new and untrustworthy.
Typing is faster, tidier and far easier to search than writing things out by hand.
Unable to capture every word, those who take notes by hand summarise and so remember more.
The slow shaping of letters by hand appears to assist young children in learning to read.
A handwritten letter feels deeply personal because it takes time and cannot be mass-produced.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(每种书写技术起初都被质疑,含笔本身) S2→B(打字更快整洁可搜索) S3→C(手写笔记须概括,记得更多) S4→D(手写字母帮助儿童学阅读) S5→E(手写信费时不可量产而显个人)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Tiny fragments of plastic, far too small to be seen with the naked eye, now reach the deepest ocean trenches and the most remote polar ice. The story of microplastics is, above all, a story of how something individually trivial can, through sheer accumulation, become quietly overwhelming. What began as a convenience material has ended as a pollutant that has spread, almost unnoticed, into very nearly every corner of the planet.

[B] Their origins are varied and often surprising. Some microplastics are produced as larger plastic items are slowly broken down by sunlight and the grinding action of the waves; others are shed in their thousands by synthetic clothes with every single wash, carried out through drains into rivers and seas; and still others were deliberately added, as tiny beads, to cosmetics and cleaning products before such uses began to be banned.

[C] Once they reach the sea, these particles are very easily mistaken for food. Creatures ranging from the smallest plankton to the largest whales swallow them, often in great quantities, and as smaller animals are eaten by larger ones, the plastic is passed steadily up the food chain. Through that same chain, some of it eventually returns, unwelcome, to our own plates and our own bodies.

[D] The health effects of all this remain genuinely uncertain. Scientists have now detected microplastics in human blood, in the lungs and in other organs, which is unsettling enough in itself, but they cannot yet say with any real confidence what harm, if any, these particles actually do once they are inside us. The honest answer, for now, is that we simply do not know.

[E] Cleaning the particles up, moreover, is very nearly impossible. Once they have dispersed through the vast and constantly moving volume of the world's oceans, the fragments are far too small and far too thinly scattered to be gathered up by any practical means. There is no net fine enough, and no operation large enough, to filter an entire sea.

[F] Prevention, therefore, matters far more than any imagined cure. Reducing the amount of plastic produced at the source, redesigning products so they shed fewer fibres, and filtering wastewater before it ever reaches the rivers all offer far more genuine hope than any heroic attempt to clean the oceans after the fact.

[G] Microplastics, in the end, force upon us an uncomfortable lesson: that the pollution we cannot even see may turn out to be the very hardest of all to undo. By the time such invisible pollution is finally noticed and properly understood, it has usually already spread far beyond any realistic hope of recovery, leaving only the slow and unglamorous discipline of prevention as a remedy. That lesson, though it arrives uncomfortably late, is nonetheless one that we can still, even now, choose to act upon.
statements: Stopping plastic at its source offers far more genuine hope than trying to clean the oceans.
Microplastics come from broken-down litter, washed synthetic clothing and added cosmetic beads.
Sea creatures mistake the fragments for food, and through the food chain they reach human plates.
Researchers have found microplastics inside the body but cannot yet confirm what harm they do.
Once spread through the oceans, the particles are too small and scattered ever to be collected.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(源头减塑比清理海洋更有望) S2→B(来自碎裂垃圾/洗涤合成衣/化妆品微珠) S3→C(海洋生物误食,经食物链入人盘) S4→D(体内已发现但危害未确认) S5→E(分散后太小太散难收集)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] The rapid rise of the so-called 'gig economy', in which people are paid for single discrete tasks rather than holding steady, salaried jobs, has divided public opinion as sharply as almost any recent change to the world of work. To some it represents liberation; to others, a quiet erosion of hard-won protections. The truth, as so often, depends a great deal on where in the new arrangement a particular worker happens to stand.

[B] For its enthusiastic champions, the central appeal is freedom. Workers can in principle choose their own hours, accept or decline whatever work they please, fit their earning around the demands of family or study, and answer to no permanent boss looking over their shoulder. For those who value autonomy above security, this flexibility can feel like a genuine and welcome escape from the rigid routines of the traditional office.

[C] Critics, however, see exactly the same arrangement in a very different and far darker light. Without sick pay, paid holidays, pensions or any guarantee of future work, gig workers are left to shoulder personally the very risks that stable employers once quietly absorbed on their behalf. The flexibility prized by the fortunate can, for the vulnerable, amount to little more than insecurity dressed up in attractive language.

[D] The platforms themselves, meanwhile, occupy a remarkably clever and profitable middle ground. They direct, monitor and rate their workers closely, exercising much of the control a traditional employer would, yet they simultaneously classify those same workers as independent contractors, thereby neatly avoiding the legal duties and costs that would otherwise be owed to genuine employees.

[E] Unpredictable income is another persistent and serious worry. Earnings can swing wildly and without warning from one week to the next, depending on demand, competition and the opaque decisions of an algorithm, making it extremely hard for a gig worker to plan ahead, to borrow money, or simply to feel any settled sense of financial security.

[F] Some governments are now beginning to respond to these concerns. They are drafting new laws designed to grant gig workers a floor of basic protections, such as a minimum wage or sick pay, without entirely removing the very flexibility that many of those workers genuinely do value and wish to keep.

[G] The deeper question beneath all the argument is whether flexibility and security must really be opposites at all, or whether work might yet be reimagined, with sufficient will, to offer ordinary people a fair measure of both at once. How different societies finally answer that question, in their laws and their everyday habits alike, will quietly shape the working lives of many millions of people for a generation or more to come. It is, in truth, less a narrow technical problem than a question about what we owe one another.
statements: Lawmakers are now drafting rules to grant gig workers a basic floor of protection.
Supporters prize the freedom to set their own hours and answer to no permanent boss.
Lacking sick pay and pensions, gig workers carry risks that employers once absorbed.
Platforms tightly direct workers yet label them independent to escape employers' duties.
Wildly unpredictable weekly earnings make it hard to plan ahead or feel secure.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(政府立法给零工基本保障底线) S2→B(自由定时间、无固定老板) S3→C(无病假养老金,承担原雇主吸收的风险) S4→D(平台严控却归类为独立以规避义务) S5→E(收入剧烈波动难规划与安全感)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] For much of the last century, raising a child with two languages at once was widely thought to confuse the young mind and to hold back its development. Parents were often warned that a second tongue would muddle the first. Modern research, accumulated patiently over recent decades, has turned that old and confident belief almost completely on its head.

[B] Far from causing harmful confusion, bilingualism actually appears to sharpen certain powers of the mind. A person who speaks two languages must, in every conversation, constantly select the right words from one language while actively suppressing the other, and this perpetual mental juggling quietly exercises the brain's machinery for controlling attention, much as regular training strengthens a muscle. This hidden mental workout goes on quietly, in every single conversation, entirely beneath the level of conscious awareness, and over many years its small daily effects appear genuinely to accumulate.

[C] The benefits, intriguingly, may last across a whole lifetime. Some careful long-term studies suggest that people who have been bilingual since childhood go on to develop the first noticeable symptoms of dementia several years later, on average, than otherwise similar people who have only ever spoken a single language, as if the mental exercise built up a protective reserve.

[D] There are rich social gains too, quite apart from any effect on the brain. A second language swings open a door into another entire culture, its literature, its humour and its habits of thought, allowing a person to see the same world through more than one set of words and to meet others, as it were, on their own ground.

[E] Old myths, however, die remarkably hard. Even now, anxious parents are still sometimes advised, by people who ought to know better, to drop the family's home language altogether so that a child can supposedly 'focus' all of its energy on mastering the dominant majority language instead.

[F] Such advice, researchers now stress with some firmness, is simply mistaken. The brains of children are perfectly well able to manage two languages at the same time without strain, and deliberately abandoning one of them rarely does the slightest good while often causing real and lasting harm to a child's confidence and family ties. A home language quietly given up in early childhood, moreover, is frequently extraordinarily difficult for that same person ever to recover fully in later life, so that what is carelessly lost may well be lost for good.

[G] Bilingualism, properly understood, is therefore not a burden to be carefully managed or apologised for, but rather a considerable gift to be actively encouraged, for the benefit of the individual child and of the wider society alike. In an increasingly connected world, the easy ability to move between languages is fast becoming less a rare luxury than a plain practical advantage.
statements: Researchers insist that abandoning a child's home language seldom helps and often does harm.
Continually choosing between two languages trains the brain's control of attention.
Some long-term studies tie lifelong bilingualism to a later onset of dementia symptoms.
A second language opens a door into another culture and a second way of seeing the world.
Parents are still occasionally told to drop the home language so a child can 'focus'.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(放弃母语很少有益常有害) S2→B(在两语间不断选择锻炼注意力控制) S3→C(终身双语者痴呆症状更晚出现) S4→D(第二语言打开另一文化与视角之门) S5→E(仍有人劝放弃家庭语言以'专注')。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] As the world's cities swell and good farmland steadily shrinks, an idea once dismissed as fanciful is now being taken seriously by engineers and investors alike: growing food not on distant open fields but inside the city itself, stacked in dense layers under carefully tuned artificial light. The notion of the indoor farm has moved, in a few short years, from science fiction toward commercial reality.

[B] The promise of the approach is genuinely striking. Vertical farms can produce fresh vegetables right where they are to be eaten, in the heart of the city, dramatically cutting the long distances food must usually travel and delivering crops to the table within mere hours of harvest rather than after days or weeks in transit. Freshness, for once, need not be sacrificed to distance.

[C] Such farms are also remarkably frugal with two precious resources. Growing crops upward in stacked trays uses only a small fraction of the ground area that an equivalent open field would demand, and the carefully sealed, recirculating water systems they rely on can recover and reuse the great majority of the water that ordinary agriculture would simply lose to the soil and the air.

[D] Yet the method has a heavy and inescapable appetite of its own. Replacing the entirely free energy of natural sunlight with banks of powerful electric lamps makes these farms genuinely expensive to run, and means that they are only ever as clean and as green as the electricity supply that happens to power them. A vertical farm fed by dirty power is no environmental triumph at all. The hungry lamps must run for many hours every single day, and the large quantities of electricity they steadily swallow have, after all, to be generated somewhere, by some means, and at some real cost.

[E] Their useful range, moreover, is presently rather narrow. Leafy greens, soft herbs and a handful of similar crops thrive happily under the lamps, but the great staple crops on which humanity actually depends for its calories, such as wheat, rice and maize, remain stubbornly and uneconomically unsuited to being grown this way.

[F] For the foreseeable future, then, vertical farming is best understood as a useful complement to traditional agriculture rather than any kind of replacement for it: a welcome local source of fresh salad greens, perhaps, but emphatically not a solution to the vast problem of feeding the whole world.

[G] Its real test, in the longer run, will be whether steadily falling costs and ever-cleaner energy can one day meaningfully widen the narrow range of crops that these strange, glowing indoor fields are able to grow. Until that distant day actually arrives, however, their bright stacked shelves seem likely to remain an interesting niche rather than the sweeping agricultural revolution their boldest champions so confidently promise.
statements: Vertical farming is best regarded for now as a complement to ordinary farming rather than a full replacement.
Growing food within the city sharply cuts transport and brings it to the table within hours.
Stacked indoor growing needs only a fraction of the land and recycles most of its water.
Substituting electric lamps for sunlight makes these farms costly and only as clean as their power.
Leafy greens flourish indoors, but staples such as wheat and rice remain unsuited to the method.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(目前是补充而非替代) S2→B(城内种植减运输、数小时内送达) S3→C(垂直种植省地、循环大部分水) S4→D(以灯替阳光成本高、只与电力同样清洁) S5→E(叶菜适室内,小麦水稻不适合)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] As genuinely self-driving cars edge ever closer to our ordinary roads, they raise a profound question that no engineer can answer alone in a laboratory: when an accident becomes unavoidable and some harm is certain, how exactly should a machine be made to choose? The hardest problems of automation, it turns out, are not mechanical at all but moral.

[B] Supporters of the technology begin with a powerful and sobering fact. The overwhelming majority of road deaths today are caused by ordinary human error, by drivers who are tired, distracted, drunk or simply careless. Machines that never grow weary, never lose their temper and never glance at a phone could, in time, prevent a great many of these tragedies and save many thousands of lives each year. The potential gain in sheer safety is, by almost any honest measure, simply enormous, and it forms the single strongest argument that the technology's advocates can make.

[C] But it is the rare and terrible dilemmas that trouble our consciences the most. If a crash genuinely cannot be avoided, should a car be programmed to protect its own passenger at absolutely any cost, or instead to minimise the total harm done to everyone involved, even if that means sacrificing its own owner? There is no obviously right answer, and reasonable people disagree.

[D] Such choices, crucially, cannot simply be left to chance or improvisation. Whatever a car actually does in that split second of crisis will inevitably reflect a rule that was written, calmly and deliberately, long in advance by some human designer sitting at a desk. The ethics are decided not on the road but in the office, years before the accident.

[E] Questions of responsibility grow correspondingly murky. When a self-driving car does cause harm, where exactly should the blame fall: on the passenger who was merely a passenger, on the manufacturer who built the vehicle, or on the programmer who quietly wrote the rules that determined its fatal decision? Our existing ideas of fault fit the situation poorly.

[F] Public trust, in the end, may matter most of all. People are extremely unlikely to accept, or to share the roads with, cars whose life-and-death decisions in a crisis they can neither understand nor had any say whatsoever in shaping. Acceptance will depend not only on safety but on a sense of fairness and transparency.

[G] The hardest part of automating the car, it gradually becomes clear, is not the driving at all, which engineers are steadily mastering, but the deciding, which belongs to all of us. These are moral choices that a whole society ought to make openly and together, through honest public debate, rather than quietly delegating them to a small handful of engineers working behind closed doors.
statements: People are unlikely to accept cars whose crisis decisions they can neither understand nor influence.
Since most crashes spring from human error, tireless machines could save many lives.
In a crash that cannot be avoided, a car must be made to choose whom to protect.
A car's split-second action reflects a rule a human designer wrote calmly in advance.
When a self-driving car causes harm, exactly who should be blamed becomes unclear.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(公众难接受无法理解或影响其危机决策的车) S2→B(多数事故源于人为失误,不知疲倦的机器可救命) S3→C(不可避免的撞车须选择保护谁) S4→D(瞬间动作反映设计者预先冷静写的规则) S5→E(致害责任归属不清)。A/G 未匹配。
